opening_technique: Camera lingers in semi-darkness on a stone surface, panning slowly across faint, ambiguous carvings—shadows flicker over one partially obscured figure, hands reaching but frozen, creating a palpable sense of dread before context emerges.
visual_motif: A fractured or shattered family heirloom—such as a broken stone tablet or split golden medallion—that appears in various states (cracked, hidden, mended, or split further) in each scene, embodying the legacy of generational trauma.
camera_language: During moments of paranoia or suspicion → handheld, slightly shaky close tracking shots that never give a full view of the subject; When characters defy their parents → low-angle shots with the background fractured or split by foreground objects; In scenes of apparent victory → wide, static shots that emphasize distance between figures; Whenever the motif appears → slow push-in to linger on the object, withholding full context until later.
forbidden_elements: No golden-hour heroic lighting—this story is not a triumph.
No slow-motion battle sequences or grand war montages—aestheticized violence undermines the trauma focus.
No Olympian thrones or epic vistas as establishing shots—the world should feel claustrophobic, not grand.
No warm familial reunions—the tone is uneasy, not redemptive.
No overtly mythological props like thunderbolts or winged sandals unless distorted or broken—the divine must feel wounded.
No voice-over exposition or text overlays in the opening—the dread must be felt, not explained.
